title: Cat Licked Topical Pain Relief Cream: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat licked human pain relief cream, act quickly. Learn why medications like Diclofenac or Flurbiprofen are highly toxic to cats.
content_html:
What is the risk?

Many human topical pain relief creams contain Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs) such as diclofenac, flurbiprofen, or ibuprofen. While safe for humans, cats are biologically unable to process these chemicals efficiently. Even a small amount licked from a human's skin or the tube can lead to severe poisoning, causing acute kidney failure or life-threatening stomach ulcers.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is an immediate medical emergency. Do not wait for your cat to show signs of illness. Symptoms of toxicity often do not appear until significant internal damage has already occurred. Immediate veterinary intervention is the best way to ensure a positive outcome for your pet.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear photo of the product's packaging and ingredient list is one of the most helpful things you can do. This allows your veterinarian or a poison control specialist to identify the exact concentration of active ingredients. Different medications require different treatment protocols, and having the label information ensures your cat receives the specific care they need without delay.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of pain cream poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Symptoms can include vomiting, lethargy, drooling, loss of appetite, and in severe cases, seizures or collapse. However, internal damage can occur before these signs are visible.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my cat vomit at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting in a cat unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian, as it can cause further injury or aspiration.

faqs.question: What if it was just a tiny lick?
faqs.answer: Even a single lick of certain creams, particularly those containing flurbiprofen, has been documented to cause fatal kidney failure in cats. Treat any amount of ingestion as a high-priority emergency.
